title: Fitting a distribution to Value-at-Risk and Expected Shortfall, with an application to covered bonds
summary: Covered bonds are a specific example of senior secured debt. If the issuer of the bonds defaults the proceeds of the assets in the cover pool are used for their debt service. If in this situation the cover pool proceeds do not suffice for the debt service, the creditors of the bonds have recourse to the issuer's assets and their claims are pari passu with the claims of the creditors of senior unsecured debt. Historically, covered bonds have been very safe investments. During their more than two hundred years of existence, investors never suffered losses due to missed payments from covered bonds. From a risk management perspective, therefore modelling covered bonds losses is mainly of interest for estimating the impact that the asset encumbrance by the cover pool has on the loss characteristics of the issuer's senior unsecured debt. We explore one-period structural modelling approaches for covered bonds and senior unsecured debt losses with one and two asset value variables respectively. Obviously, two-assets models with separate values of the cover pool and the issuer's remaining portfolio allow for more realistic modelling. However, we demonstrate that exact calibration of such models may be impossible. We also investigate a one-asset model in which the riskiness of the cover pool is reflected by a risk-based adjustment of the encumbrance ratio of the issuer's assets.

Dirk TascheвИЧ
Covered bonds are a speciпђБc example of senior secured debt. If the issuer of the bonds
defaults the proceeds of the assets in the cover pool are used for their debt service. If in
this situation the cover pool proceeds do not suпђГce for the debt service, the creditors of the
bonds have recourse to the issuerвАЩs assets and their claims are pari passu with the claims of
the creditors of senior unsecured debt. Historically, covered bonds have been very safe invest-
ments. During their more than two hundred years of existence, investors never suпђАered losses
due to missed payments from covered bonds. From a risk management perspective, therefore
modelling covered bonds losses is mainly of interest for estimating the impact that the asset
encumbrance by the cover pool has on the loss characteristics of the issuerвАЩs senior unsecured
debt. We explore one-period structural modelling approaches for covered bonds and senior un-
secured debt losses with one and two asset value variables respectively. Obviously, two-assets
models with separate values of the cover pool and the issuerвАЩs remaining portfolio allow for
more realistic modelling. However, we demonstrate that exact calibration of such models may
be impossible. We also investigate a one-asset model in which the riskiness of the cover pool
is reпђВected by a risk-based adjustment of the encumbrance ratio of the issuerвАЩs assets.
Keywords: Covered bonds, expected loss, asset value distribution, quantile, expected short-
fall, method of moments, two-parameter distribution family.
1. Introduction
Covered bonds are special debt instruments 1 that are normally served by their issuer. But if the issuer
defaults and is no longer able to make the payments due to the covered bonds holders, the debt service
is continued on the basis of the proceeds of the assets in the pool covering the bonds. If in the case of
the bond issuerвАЩs default the proceeds of the cover pool assets do not suпђГce for the debt service and the
bond holders claims are not met by the liquidation of the assets, the bond holders still can take recourse
to the remaining assets of the issuer. Their claims are then pari passu with the claims of the holders of
the issuerвАЩs senior unsecured debt.

Thus the covered bonds holders have a privileged position compared to most other of the issuerвАЩs creditors.
Indeed, no loss due to missed payments was ever observed for European covered bonds (EPRS, 2015).
While this is good news for covered bonds investors, it makes estimation of expected loss for covered
bonds diпђГcult.
Covered bonds have been popular investments for a long time especially in Europe. As a consequence of
the recent пђБnancial crisis, covered bonds became an even more important funding instrument for banks, as
such replacing instruments like securitised products. Therefore, asset encumbrance in the banksвАЩ balance
sheets due to the higher demand of collateral is increasing. This has led to concerns that expected loss for
creditors of senior unsecured debt is also increasing (CGFS, 2013). But it is not clear how to eпђГciently
quantify this eпђАect for the purpose of loss provisioning or stress testing.
This paper draws on recent work on пђБtting lognormal asset value distributions to given probability of
default (PD) and loss-given-default (LGD) estimates (Yang, 2015) and on asset encumbrance (Chan-
Lau and Oura, 2014) to suggest simple and intuitive models for covered bonds that allows quantitative
assessment of expected loss and the impact of asset encumbrance.
We пђБnd that one-period lognormal two-assets model in some situations cannot exactly be calibrated even
if diпђАerent time horizons for bond issuer and covered bond holders are taken into account. The proposed
adjusted lognormal one-asset model represents an acceptable workaround for the calibration issues, but
only as long as the credit quality fo the cover pool is only moderately better than the bond issuerвАЩs credit
quality.
The paper is organised as follows:
вАҐ In Section 2, we revisit the PD and LGD пђБtting observation by Yang (2015), proving that there is
a unique solution for the two model parameters and placing the approach in the general context of
the method of moments.
вАҐ In Section 3, we extend the lognormal one-asset model by Chan-Lau and Oura (2014) to a lognormal
two-assets model and provide formulae for eпђГcient numerical computation of expected losses for
covered bonds, senior unsecured debt and junior debt with this model.
вАҐ Computations for the two-assets model are straightforward when the model parameters are known.
However, we show in Section 4 that the calibration of the parameters leads to a non-linear equation
system that may have no exact solution at all.
вАҐ In Section 5, we revisit the one-asset model by Chan-Lau and Oura (2014). We adjust the model
for portfolio heterogeneity in terms of risk by modifying the asset encumbrance ratio related to the
covered bonds to reпђВect the expected loss of the cover pool. We show that in the case of a homoge-
neous portfolio the adjusted lognormal one-asset model is equivalent to a comonotonic special case
of the two-assets model discussed in Section 3. Unsurprisingly, therefore, exact calibration of the
model again is not always possible.
вАҐ In Section 6, we provide some numerical examples illustrating the impact of asset dependence, of
asset encumbrance, and of approximating the two-assets model with the adjusted one-asset model.
вАҐ The paper concludes with some comments in Section 7.
вАҐ Appendix A provides background information on mean-variance matching.

2. Quantile-Expected Shortfall matching
The Method of Moments 2 probably is the most popular approach to пђБtting a distribution to a пђБnite set

3. A simple model of covered bonds losses
There is not much literature on mathematical pricing or credit loss models for covered bonds.
вАҐ A detailed pricing model based on time-continuous modelling of defaults and replacements of de-
faulted assets in the cover pool is presented by Kenyon (2009).
вАҐ A one-period model for recovery rates from a bank portfolio consisting of one pool of encumbered
assets and one pool of unencumbered assets is discussed by Chan-Lau and Oura (2014, Section II D).
Chan-Lau and Oura model the value of the whole bank portfolio by one random variable. This
implies an assumption of homogeneity between the encumbered and the unencumbered pools such
that both sub-portfolios can be modelled with a single asset value variable.
Driven by the problems with calibration of the model in the absence of proper calibration data (i.e.
default and loss observations for covered bonds), we follow here the simple approach of Chan-Lau and
Oura (2014) but introduce several reпђБnements:
вАҐ We start in this section with a model with two asset value variables: one for the encumbered pool
(e.g. the cover pool for covered bonds) and one for the unencumbered pool, i.e. the part of the
portfolio which complements the cover pool.
вАҐ We then assume that the bankвАЩs total debt consists of three components: secured (by the cover pool,
overcollateralised) debt, senior unsecured debt, and junior unsecured debt. Covered bonds usually
are senior secured debt: In case of the cover pool being insuпђГcient for the service of the covered
bonds, the uncovered claim from the covered bonds debt is pari passu with the senior unsecured
debt and higher-ranking than the junior unsecured debt.
вАҐ In addition, in Section 5 we reпђБne the one-asset model of Chan-Lau and Oura (2014) by an adjust-
ment of the encumbrance ratio in order to take into account the risk proпђБle of the cover pool and
also study the three components debt structure described above in the two-assets case.
3.1. One-period, two assets approach
In this paper, we only study the case where both senior and junior (i.e. subordinated) debt are present
in the portfolio of the covered bonds issuer. From the formulae of that case, the formulae for the case
without junior debt are readily obtained by setting S = U and U = 0 in the following equations. In
mathematical terms, the setting is as follows:
вАҐ C is the amount of senior secured (by the cover pool) debt in the issuerвАЩs balance sheet. S is the
amount of senior unsecured debt. U is the amount of remaining debt, assumed to be subordinated
unsecured debt.
вАҐ v is the level of over-collateralization of the covered bonds. v = 20% means that the value of the
collateral is 120% of the face value of the covered bonds. For regulated covered bonds, in general
there is a minimum requirement of over-collateralisation. Below, the parameter v is used for model
calibration.
вАҐ X is the future value of the encumbered cover pool assets (securing the covered bonds), Y is the
future value of the remaining assets, andZ =X +Y is the future total value of the issuerвАЩs portfolio.
As asset values,X andY cannot be negative. We consider the values ofX,Y andZ as unpredictable
and, therefore, treat them as random variables.

If the credit quality of the cover pool assets is much better than the credit quality of the bond issuerвАЩs
remaining assets, (3.2) can be a major restriction for the two-assets model because it implies that the
issuerвАЩs PD cannot become arbitrarily bad (see Section 4.1 for an illustrative example). To be more precise:
Note that by (3.1c), the standalone (i.e. without support by the bond issuer) probability of loss of the cover
pool is P[X <C]. Hence, if the credit quality of the cover pool is good (i.e. P[X <C] is small) and S +U
is small compared to C (i.e. most of the пђБrmвАЩs portfolio is pledged as collateral for the covered bonds)
then (3.2) implies that the bond issuerвАЩs PD is also small even if the other assets are very risky.
In any case, it is important to keep in mind that the probabilities in (3.2) may refer to diпђАerent time
horizons, depending on the purpose the model is deployed for:
вАҐ If the model is used for calculating (say) one-year PD and LGD for the bond issuer, P[Z <C+S+U]
refers to the issuerвАЩs one-year PD while P[X <C +S +U] should be related to the maturity of the
covered bonds to which the cover pool assets serve as collateral. For only after the cover pool assets
are no longer pledged it will become clear to which extent the issuerвАЩs unsecured creditors can take
recourse to the cover pool assets to satisfy their claims.
вАҐ If the model is used for calculating (say) one-year expected loss for the covered bonds, both P[Z <
C +S +U] and P[X <C +S +U] must refer to a one-year time horizon. For in this case a covered
bonds loss event can only occur if the bond issuer defaults. Default of the covered bonds then is
triggered by the value of the cover pool after one year. The covered bonds recovery value after one
year is determined by the one-year values of the cover pool assets and the remaining assets.

4. Calibration of the two-assets model
The credit risk of a пђБnancial institution typically is stated in terms of the institutionвАЩs PD and LGD.
As described in Section 2, then often a distribution of the institutionвАЩs assets value can be пђБtted by
a variation of the method of moments such that the given PD and LGD are matched (see (2.1a) and
(2.1b)). However, in order to implement a two-assets model like in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, we need the joint
distribution of the pair (X,Y ) of the values of the cover pool and of the remaining assets respectively.
4.1. The lognormal case
In the case of the two-assets lognormal model as speciпђБed by (3.3), we have to determine the values of
the пђБve parameters ¬µ,ѕГ , ќљ,ѕД and ѕ±. It would be nice if there were a way to split the PD and LGD (or
equivalently the PD and expected loss) associated with a пђБnancial institution that issues covered bonds
into separate PD and LGD estimates for the cover pool and the pool of the remaining assets respectively.
Then we could use the approach from Section 2.2 to пђБt lognormal marginal distributions of the cover pool
assets and other assets respectively (i.e. the parameters ¬µ,ѕГ , ќљ,ѕД ) and either try to somehow estimate
the correlation ѕ± or just choose an appropriate value for ѕ±. As there is an obvious worst case (the case
ѕ± = 1 with comonotonic marginal distributions), choosing ѕ± might be a better approach than estimating
it because this way the model easily can be made to err on the conservative side.
Without further constraints, it is likely that there are many ways of splitting the issuerвАЩs PD and LGD
into PD and LGD parameters of the sub-portfolios, and some of these ways might even be reasonable
from an economic perspective. But some constraints actually make sense. Given that regulatory approval

the cover pool is likely to be subject to a number of rules regarding its composition and riskiness.

6. Numerical examples
Due to the total lack of credit loss data related to covered bonds, we cannot directly test the models
described in Sections 3.2, 4.1 and 5.1. What we can do is checking model results for economic plausibility
and comparing the margins-calibrated two-assets and adjusted one-asset models. Hence, in this section,
we present some results to illustrate how the models work in the following three scenarios:
вАҐ Increasing correlation between the values of the cover pool assets and the issuerвАЩs other assets
respectively.
вАҐ Increasing asset encumbrance by increasing the proportion of the total debt consisting of covered
bonds.
вАҐ Using the adjusted lognormal one-asset model instead of the lognormal two-assets model.
6.1. Impact of dependence between cover pool and remaining assets
We use the margins-calibrated lognormal two-assets model as described in DeпђБnition 5.5 to illustrate the
impact of correlation on the expected loss of covered bonds (senior secured debt), senior unsecured debt
and junior (unsecured) debt respectively. The input parameters are as follows:

7. Conclusions
Due to the growing importance of covered bonds as a funding tool for banks and as investment for
risk-averse investors, quantitative approaches to expected loss for covered bonds and the impact of as-
set encumbrance by covered bonds on expected loss for senior unsecured debt are of high interest for
practitioners and researchers.
In this paper, we have studied a natural lognormal two-assets model for the expected loss of covered
bonds. It has turned out, however, that in some circumstances exact calibration of this model is impos-
sible. Therefore, we have suggested a simple and computationally eпђГcient adjusted lognormal one-asset
approach to these issues. Input data requirements by this approach are light: PD and LGD of the covered
bonds issuer, expected loss of the cover pool as well as the distribution of debt types in the issuerвАЩs
23
portfolio and the level of over-collateralisation of the covered bonds. The model therefore can be applied
in situations like stress testing where little information is available and many bonds have to be evaluated
in short time.
We have demonstrated that for homogeneous portfolios the one-asset approach is a special case of the
more natural two-assets approach whose calibration, however, is diпђГcult and sometimes not feasible at
all. Backtesting of the two models discussed is impossible because never any covered bonds defaults
were observed. Nonetheless, we have presented numerical examples that suggest that the results by the
proposed вАЩadjusted lognormal one-asset modelвАЩ concur with intuition as long as the risk levels of the
issuerвАЩs entire portfolio and of the cover pool of the covered bonds are not materially diпђАerent.
